data.title: XED, India's first GIFT city IPO, withdraws issue after weak demand
data.description:
XED Executive Development (XEDE.NS), the first company from India's low-tax GIFT City to launch an initial public offering, has withdrawn the IPO after delays with customer verification and weak market sentiments due to the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, it said on Monday.

The global executive education platform said it hopes to tap the market at an appropriate time in the future.

The withdrawal marks a setback for efforts to build India's Gujarat International Finance Tec-City, or GIFT City, into a global capital markets hub competing with centres such as Singapore and Dubai.

The roughly $12 million IPO of XED had received subscriptions for only about 5% of the shares on offer as of 7:15 p.m. on Monday, according to data from exchanges.

The shares were to be listed on NSE International Exchange and India International Exchange at GIFT City.

The company had earlier extended the bidding deadline to Monday, citing delays in completing mandatory video-based customer verification for non-resident Indians and foreign investors amid disruptions linked to the conflict in the Middle East.

The withdrawal also comes amid a global risk-off mood due to the widening conflict.

($1 = 94.7150 Indian rupees)

data.title: Treasury Yields Could be Peaking as Oil Rise Threatens Growth — Market Talk
data.description: Yields are likely around their ceiling, JPMorgan analysts write. They expect a further increase in oil prices to make the Fed more dovish, considering the threat to economic growth. With markets mostly pricing a prolonged hold on interest rates, "the front end looks cheap after controlling for the rise in energy prices," JPMorgan says. "We maintain our bullish bias at the front end and continue to hold longs in 2-year Treasuries." Risks to this scenario increase as the conflict extends over time, they say. Both the two-year and the 10-year Treasury yields are on path to end March with their largest increase since October 2024. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Japanese Yen's Scope to Fall Looks Limited — Market Talk
data.description: The Japanese yen has limited room to weaken further given the potential for interventions to prop up the currency and for risk aversion to rise on the Iran war, MUFG Bank analysts say in a note. Any renewed yen selling will likely be met by interventions as threatened by Japanese authorities, they say. If global growth concerns intensify then global equities will likely suffer bigger falls, pushing front-end U.S. yields lower and prompting a liquidation of long positions betting on a stronger dollar versus the yen, they say. The dollar falls 0.6% to 159.38 yen after Japan's top currency diplomat Atsushi Mimura warned of possible interventions. The dollar reached a 20-month high of 160.46 yen overnight, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: DMYD_B: Phase III interim results showed futility for retogatein in type 1 diabetes, prompting urgent review
data.description:
Interim phase III results for retogatein in type 1 diabetes showed futility, with no efficacy observed in any subgroup, contradicting prior positive data. A thorough investigation is underway, and the trial continues until the root cause is determined. Cash position remains strong at $40 million.

data.title: Raw sugar turns lower after hitting five-month peak
data.description:
Raw sugar futures on ICE turned lower on Monday after earlier climbing to a five-month high while robusta coffee prices sank to a 7-1/2 month low.

SUGAR

* Raw sugar was down 0.8% at 15.63 cents per lb by 1359 GMT, having earlier hit a five-month high of 16.10 cents.

* Dealers noted a modest pullback was not unexpected given the steep recent run-up in prices. The market has risen as much as 18% since early March.

* Higher energy prices and the rising cost of fertiliser, both linked to conflict in the Middle East, have helped to drive the sugar market higher.

* White sugar fell 1% to $453.90 a metric ton.

COFFEE

* Robusta coffee lost 3.5% to $3,468 a ton after slumping to a 7-1/2 month low of $3,463.

* Dealers said the weather remained generally favourable in top robusta producer Vietnam while a bumper coffee crop is expected in Brazil this year.

* Cooxupe, Brazil's largest coffee cooperative, expects to receive a total of 6.8 million 60-kg coffee bags from its members and third parties this year, up nearly 12% from 2025, officials said on Friday.

* Arabica coffee fell 2.8% to $2.9355 per lb.

* Italian coffee maker Lavazza on Monday posted an increase in annual revenue and profit despite persistently high bean prices and weaker global demand.

COCOA

* London cocoa ​lost 1% to 2,346 pounds a ton.

* Dealers said prices were dropping towards the lower end of the market's recent range, with weak demand remaining a concern.

* They noted that the market would keep a close watch on first-quarter grind data. European and North American reports are both scheduled to be issued on April 16.

* New York cocoa was down 1.4% at $3,122 a ton.

* Above-average rainfall across most of Ivory Coast's key cocoa-growing regions last week has boosted expectations for a longer and more productive March-to-August mid-crop, farmers said on Monday.

data.title: China National Building Material Company: Net loss of RMB3.7 billion on lower revenue, with new materials segment showing strong growth
data.description:
Revenue declined 1.9% to RMB177.8 billion with a net loss of RMB3.7 billion, driven by weak demand, heavy impairments, and lower prices in core segments, while new materials and engineering technology services showed growth. Final dividend proposed at RMB0.15 per share.

Original document: China National Building Material Co. Ltd. Class H [3323] Earnings Release — Mar. 30 2026
